tasks.goal:
Create the single file src/build.py. It is a Blender Python script that procedurally constructs the geometry of a simple wooden 4-leg dining chair. Geometry only — no camera, no lights, no render config. The framework will render the scene from multiple viewpoints after your script runs; if you add a camera, render settings, or call bpy.ops.render.render, those will be stripped.

Behavior, in order:

1. Start clean (this leaves scene.world=None — that's fine, do NOT touch scene.world):
     import bpy
     bpy.ops.wm.read_factory_settings(use_empty=True)

2. Construct the chair from bpy primitive ops only (no bmesh). Use a small helper like `add_box(name, location, scale)` that wraps bpy.ops.mesh.primitive_cube_add + obj.scale + obj.color. Approximate dimensions (units = meters; seat top should be at z≈0.45):
   - Seat:      40cm × 40cm × 4cm, centered at (0, 0, 0.45)
   - Four legs: 5cm × 5cm × 45cm cubes, tops at z≈0.43, one at each corner: (±0.175, ±0.175, 0.225)
   - Backrest:  38cm wide × 4cm thick × 40cm tall, centered at (0, +0.18, 0.67) so its bottom meets the back edge of the seat and it stands upright facing -Y

   Reminder for cube_add: it creates a 2m × 2m × 2m cube. Use the size= parameter or set obj.scale to get the dimensions above; e.g. for a 0.40 × 0.40 × 0.04 seat from a default 2m cube, obj.scale = (0.20, 0.20, 0.02).

3. Give every chair part a warm wood-brown color so workbench OBJECT shading shows them clearly. For each part object obj:
     obj.color = (0.45, 0.27, 0.15, 1.0)

Hard constraints:
- Use only bpy. No third-party packages, no pip installs.
- Do NOT set scene.world.<anything> — use_empty=True leaves scene.world as None.
- Do NOT add cameras or lights — the framework provides them.
- Do NOT call bpy.ops.render.render or set scene.render.* — the framework renders.
- All chair parts must touch but not overlap so the chair is structurally readable.
- Write src/build.py with the Write tool. Do not run Blender yourself.
- Keep the script self-contained and easy to read; ~40 lines is plenty.
